evidence_text: The study evaluates seven general-purpose agent frameworks on three code-centric tasks: software development, vulnerability detection, and program repair.
source_excerpt: The abstract states that the authors evaluate “seven general-purpose agent frameworks across three representative code-centric tasks: software development, vulnerability detection, and program repair.”
note:
The paper frames the comparison as cross-task and general-purpose rather than task-specific.
```
caveat:
The paper frames the comparison as cross-task and general-purpose rather than task-specific.
```

evidence_text: The benchmark suite uses SRDD with 1,200 software-development prompts, LLM-SmartAudit with 115 vulnerability instances, and SWE-bench Lite with 300 program-repair instances.
source_excerpt: Table 2 lists “SRDD ... 1,200,” “LLM-SmartAudit ... 115,” and “SWEBench-Lite ... 300,” and §3.3 states that the study selects these three benchmarks for the three tasks.
note:
The vulnerability benchmark count comes from 102 common-vulnerability contracts plus 13 CVE contracts.
```
caveat:
The vulnerability benchmark count comes from 102 common-vulnerability contracts plus 13 CVE contracts.
```

evidence_text: All frameworks were run with a maximum step limit of 100 and the same backend LLM, DeepSeek-v3.1.
source_excerpt: The experimental setup says: “the maximum step limit was set to 100” for all tasks and frameworks, and “all agents utilize the DeepSeek-v3.1 ... as their backend LLM.”
note:
The authors present this as a control for comparability and cost.
```
caveat:
The authors present this as a control for comparability and cost.
```

evidence_text: OpenHands has the highest overall software-development quality score in SRDD at 0.47.
source_excerpt: Table 4 reports “OpenHands ... Average Quality ... 0.47,” and the text states: “Based on this composite metric, OpenHands is the top-performing framework for software development tasks, achieving a quality score of 0.47.”
note:
Quality is defined as Completeness × Executability × Consistency.
```
caveat:
Quality is defined as Completeness × Executability × Consistency.
```

evidence_text: AgentOrchestra records the highest average completeness in software development at 0.86.
source_excerpt: Table 4 shows “AgentOrchestra ... Average” with completeness 0.86, and the text says “AgentOrchestra demonstrated the highest effectiveness, achieving an impressive average score of 0.86” for completeness.
note:
This evidence is specific to the completeness submetric, not the overall quality score.
```
caveat:
This evidence is specific to the completeness submetric, not the overall quality score.
```

evidence_text: OpenHands records average executability of 1.00 in software development.
source_excerpt: Table 4 lists OpenHands with executability values of 1.00 across the five categories and an average executability of 1.00. The text says OpenHands attained “perfect scores (1.00)” across categories and contrasts this with the overall average executability.
note:
This is a submetric result and does not imply highest completeness or consistency.
```
caveat:
This is a submetric result and does not imply highest completeness or consistency.
```

evidence_text: GPTswarm records the highest average consistency in software development at 0.85.
source_excerpt: Table 4 shows “GPTswarm ... Average” with consistency 0.85, and the text states: “For consistency, GPTswarm emerged as the leader, with a superior average score of 0.85.”
note:
This is reported as the leading consistency submetric among the evaluated agents.
```
caveat:
This is reported as the leading consistency submetric among the evaluated agents.
```

evidence_text: GPTswarm achieves the highest overall vulnerability-detection result at 80 detected cases out of 115, reported as 78%.
source_excerpt: Table 5 reports “GPTswarm ... Total (115) 80 (78%),” and the abstract says “GPTswarm achieving the highest detection accuracy in vulnerability analysis.”
note:
The source also contains a textual mention of 77% in some summary passages, but Table 5 reports 80/115 as 78%.
```
caveat:
The source also contains a textual mention of 77% in some summary passages, but Table 5 reports 80/115 as 78%.
```

evidence_text: Gas Limitation (GL) vulnerabilities are rarely detected by the evaluated agents.
source_excerpt: Table 5 shows GL detections of 0, 0, 0, 0, 1, 1, 2, 1, and 0 across the agents, and the text states that “for the GL vulnerability type, all seven agents demonstrated very low detection efficiency, basically failing to discover any vulnerabilities.”
note:
This evidence marks a task-specific weakness shared across the compared frameworks rather than a general statement about all vulnerability classes.
```
caveat:
This evidence marks a task-specific weakness shared across the compared frameworks rather than a general statement about all vulnerability classes.
```

evidence_text: SE-Agent (Iter-3) attains the highest overall program-repair result at 161 repaired issues out of 300, reported as 54%.
source_excerpt: Table 6 reports “SE-Agent (Iter-3) ... Total (300) 161 (54%),” and the text says “SE-Agent (Iter-3) achieved the highest accuracy, repairing 54% of the total issues.”
note:
This is the highest total repair rate in the table.
```
caveat:
This is the highest total repair rate in the table.
```

evidence_text: No evaluated agent repairs any of the 3 Flask issues in SWE-bench Lite.
source_excerpt: Table 6 shows the “flask (3)” row with “0 (0%)” for every agent, and the text says “for flask repository ... none of the agents succeeded in repairing any issue.”
note:
The paper explicitly notes this as “an unexpected outcome,” indicating that small repository issue count did not imply easier repair.
```
caveat:
The paper explicitly notes this as “an unexpected outcome,” indicating that small repository issue count did not imply easier repair.
```

evidence_text: AgentOrchestra, OWL, and GPTswarm show poor program-repair effectiveness because they do not use patch tooling and fail to generate correct diff-format patches.
source_excerpt: The text states: “AgentOrchestra, OWL, and GPTswarm exhibit distinctly poor effectiveness. The underlying reason is that they do not leverage Patch tooling ... which leads to failures in generating correct diff-format patches.”
note:
This is the authors’ stated explanation for a specific repair-performance failure mode.
```
caveat:
This is the authors’ stated explanation for a specific repair-performance failure mode.
```

evidence_text: AgentOrchestra has the highest total monetary cost across the three tasks at $370.19.
source_excerpt: Table 10 and the accompanying text state: “AgentOrchestra incurred the highest cost at $370.19 across the three tasks.”
note:
The source reports monetary overhead in USD under a common backend model setting.
```
caveat:
The source reports monetary overhead in USD under a common backend model setting.
```

evidence_text: GPTswarm has the lowest total monetary cost across the three tasks at $16.29.
source_excerpt: The overhead discussion says “GPTswarm solved all problems across the three tasks with merely $16.29,” and the summary states that “GPTswarm is the most cost-efficient.”
note:
This is the lowest total cost reported among the evaluated frameworks.
```
caveat:
This is the lowest total cost reported among the evaluated frameworks.
```

evidence_text: OpenHands consumes the most tokens across all three tasks, but its cost does not scale proportionally because cached input tokens are cheaper.
source_excerpt: The paper states that “OpenHands consumed 1.26 B input tokens and 30.54 M output tokens” in software development and also consumed the most tokens in the other two tasks. It explains the cost gap by noting that “the model can leverage a caching mechanism to reuse previously processed inputs” and that “even with a large number of input tokens, the overall cost may remain low due to the lower pricing of cached tokens.”
note: This evidence ties a high-token / not-highest-cost pattern to the paper’s caching explanation, so the cost interpretation should stay attached to that caveat.
caveat: This evidence ties a high-token / not-highest-cost pattern to the paper’s caching explanation, so the cost interpretation should stay attached to that caveat.
